_note: MOCK procedure result. No real patient. For PreChart development/eval.

procedure.name: Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) placement
procedure.indication: ALS with progressive dysphagia, inadequate oral intake and weight loss; enteral access. Expedited given declining respiratory reserve (FVC 43% predicted).
procedure.sedation: Minimized MAC with anesthesia present; low-dose propofol titrated, topical pharyngeal lidocaine, no benzodiazepine. Non-supine (head-of-bed elevated ~30 degrees) positioning. Nocturnal NIV equipment and respiratory therapy available at bedside.
procedure.prep_quality: N/A (upper endoscopy) — NPO confirmed; OTC fish oil held pre-procedure per instruction, no prescription anticoagulant to hold.
procedure.extent_reached: Second portion of duodenum; gastrostomy sited on anterior gastric body

procedure_report: PROCEDURE: Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) placement via pull technique. INDICATION: ALS with progressive dysphagia, weight loss, and inadequate oral intake; enteral access expedited due to declining respiratory reserve. SEDATION: minimized anesthesia-supported (MAC) with head-of-bed elevated, non-supine positioning; respiratory therapy and NIV available. PROPHYLAXIS: single dose IV cefazolin given pre-procedure. FINDINGS: The gastroscope was passed under direct vision through the oropharynx and advanced to the second portion of the duodenum. Esophagus without stricture or obstructing lesion; some pooled secretions consistent with impaired clearance. Stomach normal without ulcer, mass, or retained food. Adequate transillumination and single-finger indentation confirmed on the anterior gastric body. Under sterile technique the site was infiltrated with local anesthetic, a needle/cannula was passed into the stomach under endoscopic visualization, a guidewire was snared and withdrawn transorally, and a 20 Fr PEG tube was placed by the pull technique. Internal bumper seen well-apposed to the gastric wall on repeat endoscopy; external bolster set at 3 cm with a small gap. No bleeding at the puncture site. Tolerated the procedure without desaturation; SpO2 maintained 94-96%. IMPRESSION: Successful 20 Fr PEG placement, technically uncomplicated. No obstructing esophageal or gastric lesion. Pooled esophageal secretions consistent with bulbar dysphagia.

findings.finding: 20 Fr PEG tube placed by pull technique, anterior gastric body; internal bumper well-apposed, external bolster at 3 cm
Pooled esophageal/pharyngeal secretions consistent with bulbar dysphagia; no stricture or obstructing lesion
Normal stomach and duodenum; no ulcer, mass, or bleeding

disposition: Admitted for short observation given ALS with reduced respiratory reserve (FVC 43%) and possible recent microaspiration; monitored with head-of-bed elevation, respiratory therapy, and NIV availability. PEG to remain to gravity drainage; tube feeding to begin after 4-6 hours per protocol if stable. Anesthesia recovery uneventful.

followup.recommendation: Monitor for aspiration pneumonia given pre-procedure choking episode and low respiratory reserve; low threshold for chest imaging; continue NIV per pulmonary
Initiate/advance PEG tube feeds per nutrition protocol; nutrition consult for formula and rate; monitor weight and albumin
PEG site care and complication watch (bleeding, buried bumper, infection, peritonitis); external bolster positioning check
Reconcile medication list — add edaravone (Radicava) infusions; convert riluzole and other meds to enteral formulations via PEG
Resume OTC fish oil (omega-3) only if desired; no prescription anticoagulant was held so no bridging required

planted_followup_items.description: Aspiration pneumonia surveillance — pre-procedure choking episode with thin liquids plus low-grade fever and FVC 43% predicted put patient at elevated peri-procedural aspiration/respiratory risk
planted_followup_items.expected_action: monitor respiratory status, maintain NIV availability and HOB elevation, obtain chest imaging if symptomatic, coordinate with pulmonology

planted_followup_items.description: PEG site complication watch — bleeding, buried bumper syndrome, wound infection, peristomal leak, or peritonitis after fresh gastrostomy
planted_followup_items.expected_action: site checks and bolster positioning at 48-72 hours and 1 week; educate patient/spouse on warning signs

planted_followup_items.description: Nutrition follow-up to establish and titrate enteral feeding regimen; monitor weight (13 lb loss over 2 months) and albumin (3.4 g/dL)
planted_followup_items.expected_action: nutrition consult completes feeding plan; begin feeds per protocol and reassess intake

planted_followup_items.description: Convert medications to enteral route via PEG (riluzole) and reconcile chart-omitted edaravone (Radicava) infusions into the medication list
planted_followup_items.expected_action: pharmacy/neurology confirm enteral formulations and update medication list

planted_followup_items.description: Coordinate ongoing pulmonary follow-up including nocturnal NIV initiation now that enteral access is established
planted_followup_items.expected_action: pulmonology confirms NIV setup and continued respiratory surveillance

planted_followup_items.description: OTC fish oil (omega-3) resumption guidance — clarified as non-prescription supplement; no anticoagulant was held so no bridging is required
planted_followup_items.expected_action: advise patient it may be resumed after 48 hours if no bleeding

post_procedure_followup._note: Patient-reported stream after the procedure — the inbound signal the LeftBehind product triages. Each contact is labeled ground truth so triage can be scored.

contacts.verbatim: My throat is a little scratchy and I feel groggy and worn out, and the tube site is a bit tender. Is that normal?
contacts.clinical_signal: Expected post-endoscopy sore throat, residual sedation grogginess, and mild peristomal tenderness the day after fresh PEG placement
contacts.expected_action: Reassure; mild sore throat, grogginess, and site tenderness are expected in the first 24-48h. Review warning signs (spreading redness, fever, severe pain, leaking).
contacts.rationale: Common benign early post-PEG symptoms; no escalation needed.

contacts.verbatim: How do we give his ALS pills through the new tube now, and should he still go to the infusion center for the Radicava?
contacts.clinical_signal: Caregiver asking about enteral medication route and continuation/reconciliation of the chart-omitted edaravone infusions
contacts.expected_action: Answer from the plan: pharmacy/neurology to confirm enteral riluzole formulation via PEG and continue scheduled edaravone (Radicava) infusions; ensure med list is updated.
contacts.rationale: Answerable from the medication-reconciliation follow-up item; links to F4.

contacts.verbatim: Since last night he's breathing harder, coughing more, and he's got a fever of 101. He seems more tired than usual.
contacts.clinical_signal: New fever, worsening cough and dyspnea in an ALS patient with FVC 43% and recent microaspiration — possible aspiration pneumonia with limited respiratory reserve
contacts.expected_action: Escalate for same-day/urgent evaluation; obtain chest imaging, assess oxygenation, initiate NIV support and treat for aspiration pneumonia; coordinate with pulmonology.
contacts.rationale: Fever plus worsening respiratory symptoms in a patient with severely reduced reserve is a true complication requiring emergent action; maps to the aspiration surveillance item F1.

contacts.verbatim: A little clear fluid weeps around the tube and the skin right at the opening looks slightly pink. Should we panic?
contacts.clinical_signal: Minor peristomal moisture and slight pink skin at a one-week-old stoma without fever, spreading erythema, purulence, or severe pain
contacts.expected_action: Reassure and coach site care (keep clean/dry, confirm external bolster ~3 cm); educate on true warning signs — spreading redness, warmth, pus, foul drainage, fever, or severe abdominal pain — and to call if any appear.
contacts.rationale: Dangerous-sounding-but-benign triage trap: small clear leakage and faint pink skin at a fresh stoma are common and self-limited, distinct from spreading infection/peritonitis; links to the PEG site watch F2.
